when-to-walk-away-from-a-buyer-offer.title: When to Walk Away from a Buyer Offer
when-to-walk-away-from-a-buyer-offer.author: Onsite Real Estate
when-to-walk-away-from-a-buyer-offer.excerpt: It’s exciting to receive an offer on your home—but not every offer is a good one. In fact, some can lead to delays, extra costs, or failed closings. Knowing when to walk away is just as important as knowing when to accept. At Onsite Real Estate Group
when-to-walk-away-from-a-buyer-offer.body: It’s exciting to receive an offer on your home—but not every offer is a good one. In fact, some can lead to delays, extra costs, or failed closings. Knowing when to walk away is just as important as knowing when to accept. At Onsite Real Estate Group, we help sellers protect their interests by spotting red flags early. Here’s how to tell when it might be time to move on. The Buyer Is Asking for Too Many Concessions If an offer is packed with demands—like excessive seller credits, closing cost coverage, or major repair requests—it may indicate a buyer who’s not financially prepared or emotionally committed. While some concessions are normal, too many can quickly cut into your profit or delay the process. Financing Looks Unstable A weak pre-approval, a small down payment, or a lender with a reputation for slow closings can be warning signs. If the buyer hasn’t submitted earnest money or their financing terms keep changing, it may be safer to pursue a stronger, more qualified offer. Contingencies Stack Up Standard contingencies like inspection or appraisal are common—but when you start seeing “sale of buyer’s home” or other complex clauses, the risk increases. These contingencies create extra dependencies that can fall through, leaving you back at square one. Buyer Isn’t Responsive Communication matters. If the buyer (or their agent) is unresponsive during the negotiation stage, things are unlikely to improve during escrow. Lack of follow-through can signal disorganization, indecisiveness, or a lack of serious intent. Your Gut Says Something’s Off Sometimes, it’s not one big red flag—it’s a bunch of small concerns that just don’t sit right. As a seller, you have the right to prioritize a clean, smooth transaction. If something feels off, trust your instincts and consult your agent before committing. why-suburban-living-is-growing-in-washin.title: Why Suburban Living Is Growing in Washington | Onsite Real Estate
why-suburban-living-is-growing-in-washin.author: Onsite Real Estate
why-suburban-living-is-growing-in-washin.excerpt: Suburban communities across Washington are experiencing a surge in popularity. As affordability challenges in city centers persist, more buyers are seeking homes in suburban markets that offer more space, value, and lifestyle perks. Affordability Is
why-suburban-living-is-growing-in-washin.body: Suburban communities across Washington are experiencing a surge in popularity. As affordability challenges in city centers persist, more buyers are seeking homes in suburban markets that offer more space, value, and lifestyle perks. Affordability Is Driving Demand With median home prices in Seattle still among the highest in the state, buyers are turning to suburban areas like Puyallup, Bonney Lake, and Lake Tapps. These communities offer more square footage for the money while remaining commutable to major job centers. More Space for Families The pandemic reshaped how people view their living space, and the demand for larger homes, bigger yards, and home offices hasn’t gone away. Suburbs provide families with the flexibility city condos can’t match. Improved Infrastructure and Transit Options Expanding highways, Sound Transit projects, and new commercial hubs have made suburban living more convenient than ever. Buyers no longer feel they’re sacrificing accessibility when moving outside Seattle. Lifestyle and Community Appeal Suburbs often provide access to parks, lakes, and recreational amenities while maintaining a sense of community. For many Washington buyers, this balance of nature and convenience is highly attractive. As of 2025, the median home price in Seattle is $825,000 , compared to $560,000 in Pierce County (NWMLS). Suburban home searches in Washington increased by 21% year-over-year , according to Redfin. Rising Popularity Among First-Time Buyers Younger buyers who can’t afford to purchase in King County are choosing Pierce and Thurston Counties instead. Starter homes and townhouses in suburban markets are seeing significant competition. What This Means for Sellers For sellers in suburban communities, this trend is good news. Marketing homes with an emphasis on space, affordability, and lifestyle benefits can attract relocating buyers and first-time homeowners. Check your home value using our Home Value Estimator . are-home-prices-rising-or-falling-in-pie.body: If you're planning to sell or buy in 2025, one of your biggest questions is likely: "What’s happening with home prices?" The market in Pierce County is shifting—but not in the way many expected. Here’s what we’re seeing so far this year and what it means for you. Pierce County Home Prices Are Holding Strong So far in 2025, home prices across East Pierce County—including Lake Tapps, Bonney Lake, and Puyallup—have remained steady. While we’re not seeing the rapid appreciation of past years, prices aren’t falling either. Why Prices Aren’t Dropping (Yet) One of the biggest reasons? Inventory is still relatively low. Even with more homes hitting the market compared to last year, demand is keeping pace—especially for move-in-ready homes in sought-after neighborhoods. What Buyers Are Looking For Today’s buyers are looking for value. Updated kitchens, clean curb appeal, and staged homes are performing better than ever. Homes that check the right boxes are still attracting strong interest, even with higher interest rates. What This Means for Sellers This is a great time to list—if you price strategically. Buyers are no longer rushing in with aggressive offers, but they’re still motivated to buy. A well-priced home with the right presentation can still sell fast and close to asking price. How Onsite Helps You Win in 2025 We keep a constant eye on local trends and buyer behavior, so our clients get real-time advice—not outdated market data. Whether you’re selling now or planning ahead, we tailor your strategy for today’s conditions—not last year’s. Curious About Your Home’s Value? should-you-sell-your-home-before-you-buy.body: If you're planning to move, one of the biggest decisions you’ll face is whether to sell your current home before buying the next. For Pierce County homeowners, timing matters. Let’s break down your options, the pros and cons of each, and how to make the smartest move for your situation. Selling First — What It Means Selling your current home before buying gives you access to your equity right away. It reduces financial risk and can strengthen your buying power—but it also requires planning. Benefits: You know your budget for your next purchase You may have stronger leverage as a buyer (especially with cash in hand) Avoids the stress of carrying two mortgages Drawbacks: You may need short-term housing The market could shift before you find your next home Buying First — What You Need to Know Buying a home before selling offers peace of mind and a smoother transition—but only if you’re financially ready. Benefits: No pressure to find a home quickly You can move once, without temporary housing Drawbacks: You’ll need to qualify for both mortgages or bridge financing You could be left juggling two homes temporarily What Most Sellers in Pierce County Do In East Pierce County—especially Lake Tapps, Bonney Lake, and Puyallup—many sellers choose to sell first, particularly when their equity is essential for the next purchase. However, creative solutions like contingent offers or rent-back agreements can help either route work. Pro Tip — Consider a Rent-Back Agreement A rent-back allows you to stay in your home for up to 60 days after closing, giving you time to house hunt without rushing—while already having your sale funds in hand. So, What’s the Right Choice? There’s no one-size-fits-all answer. The best path depends on your finances, your goals, and the current market. That’s why working with a local expert matters. whats-the-best-time-of-year-to-sell-a-ho.body: Timing matters in real estate. If you're planning to sell your home in Pierce County, choosing the right time of year could help you attract more buyers, sell faster, and get a higher offer. Let’s take a look at when—and why—homeowners tend to get the best results. Spring Is the Hottest Season for Sellers Historically, late March through May is the best window to list your home. Buyers are active, curb appeal is at its peak, and families want to move before summer. Homes listed in spring often sell faster and closer to (or above) asking price. Summer Brings High Buyer Demand June through August can still be a great time to sell, especially in family-oriented neighborhoods. Buyers are looking to settle before the school year starts—but competition from other listings can be higher too. Fall Is Underrated But Still Active September and October offer a second wave of serious buyers. These buyers are often more motivated and looking to close before the holidays. Homes that are priced right can still move quickly this time of year. Winter Can Work With the Right Strategy While December through February is traditionally slower, inventory is lower—and that means less competition. If your home is well-staged and priced strategically, winter sales can still be strong, especially for buyers relocating to Pierce County. What Time Is Best for You? The "right" time isn’t just about seasons—it’s about your goals. Whether you’re looking to maximize profit or time your move with a purchase, we’ll help you create a personalized plan that works with your schedule and the market.
